coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of carbon quantum dots from purslane leaves for the detection of formaldehyde using quartz crystal microbalance
coredata.dc:creator: Amer, Wael A.
Rehab, Ahmed F.
Abdelghafar, Mona E.
Torad, Nagy L.
Atlam, Ahmed S.
Ayad, Mohamad M.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized by using a facile one–step hydrothermal treatment of purslane leaves in a green and economical approach. The optical and structural properties of the CQDs have been studied by using many analytical techniques. The research interest in studying the sensing properties of CQDs is based on their good properties in addition to the combination of the developed graphitic carbon and the active functional groups that present in CQDs, which collectively can lead to the detection of some analytes with good sensitivity. The results of the CQDs–based sensor, using the quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) technique, show a good detection sensitivity toward volatile hazardous formaldehyde. The QCM sensor shows the high impact of the oxygen functionalities of CQDs as sensing antennae on the good sensitivity of volatile formaldehyde via enhanced chemisorption as compared to alcohols. Based on calibration graphs of frequency shift against the vapor concentration, the linear increase of CQDs sensor responses with the increased injection of chemical vapor concentration with a high sensitivity allows CQDs to be used for the detection of formaldehyde vapors. The fabricated QCM sensor exhibits a good reproducibility and reversibility. The kinetics of formaldehyde sensing compared to other analytes vapors were discussed.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of carbon quantum dots from purslane leaves for the detection of formaldehyde using quartz crystal microbalance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized by using a facile one–step hydrothermal treatment of purslane leaves in a green and economical approach. The optical and structural properties of the CQDs have been studied by using many analytical techniques. The research interest in studying the sensing properties of CQDs is based on their good properties in addition to the combination of the developed graphitic carbon and the active functional groups that present in CQDs, which collectively can lead to the detection of some analytes with good sensitivity. The results of the CQDs–based sensor, using the quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) technique, show a good detection sensitivity toward volatile hazardous formaldehyde. The QCM sensor shows the high impact of the oxygen functionalities of CQDs as sensing antennae on the good sensitivity of volatile formaldehyde via enhanced chemisorption as compared to alcohols. Based on calibration graphs of frequency shift against the vapor concentration, the linear increase of CQDs sensor responses with the increased injection of chemical vapor concentration with a high sensitivity allows CQDs to be used for the detection of formaldehyde vapors. The fabricated QCM sensor exhibits a good reproducibility and reversibility. The kinetics of formaldehyde sensing compared to other analytes vapors were discussed.

ce:section.ce:para: Sensing of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) vapor is an area of great interest in many various applications. Formaldehyde is one of the VOCs that is resulted during the combustion of methane and other organic compounds. Formaldehyde also has been extensively utilized by organic chemists for producing resins due to its adhesive characteristics [1]. Chronic exposure to formaldehyde vapors in concentrations higher than 0.1 ppm through inhalation may lead to eye symptoms [2,3], irritation and dyspnea.

Considering the hazardous health effects of the VOCs under investigation, their detection is an ongoing challenging area. A wide variety of techniques have been developed for the design of VOCs sensors. Among them, electrochemical sensors [4,5], gas sensitive resistors [6], and gas and liquid chromatography [7,8] have been studied so far. Although these research approaches, while showing encouraging results with high accuracy and reliability, have rarely considered the issue of sensor selectivity to various volatile analytes and still suffer from limitations, which make quantitative analysis difficult, such as involvement of complicated steps [8], high cost and time-consumption [9]. Therefore, efforts are still devoted toward investigation of advanced detection systems for detecting VOCs with high sensitivity and selectivity. An increasing attention has been directed to apply quartz crystal microbalance (QCM)-based sensors for gases [10], since it was first introduced by King et al. in 1964. QCM can monitor the mass change (m’) induced by the analyte adsorbed onto the gold surface of the QCM electrode by measuring the change in frequency (Δf) of a quartz crystal based on Sauerbrey equation [11].
(1)
Δ f =
−
(
2
f o
2
/
ρ Q
μ Q
)
m `
where, ρ
Q refers the quartz density (2.649 g cm−3), ƒ o (Hz) describes the natural frequency of the quartz crystal, and μ
Q refers to the shear modulus (2.947 × 1011 dyne cm−2).

The sensitivity, selectivity, response time and reversibility of the QCM sensor depend on the physical and chemical properties of the materials coated onto the QCM gold electrode. Several polymeric coatings on QCM were used for sensor applications of VOCs due to the mass changes that arise from the interaction of these coatings with VOCs. Polyvinylpyrrolidone–coated QCM electrode was used for the determination of ammonia and various aliphatic amines vapor [12]. Furthermore, conducting polymers such as polyaniline (PANI) and PANI-TiO2 nanocomposite coated QCM electrode were exploited for trimethylamine detection [13,14] and the results showed relatively low detection sensitivity and reversibility. In our group, PANI and mesoporous silica [10,15] were exploited as a coating on the QCM electrode for the detection of alcohols, chlorinated hydrocarbons [16–18], pH [19], and phosphoric acid [20]. The frequency changes of the QCM were linearly correlated to the concentration of different analyte species and hence calibration curves were obtained. All these sensors showed a good reproducibility and reversibility.

Nanomaterials afford unique structural and functional features such as pendant functional groups, high surface area, strong adsorption capacity, as well as high chemical activity. Hence, they have become more feasible for environmental remediation and trace-level analysis of various target species [21]. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as an example of carbon nanomaterials have drawn great attention because of their unique structural features, good optical photostability, low toxicity, optical and electronic properties [22,23]. They have been extensively applied in optoelectronic devices, photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, biosensing, bioimaging, etc [24–28]. Carbon quantum dots were prepared in many investigations from different natural carbon resources such as grape, lime, watermelon, orange juices, milk, potato, plant leaves, water hyacinth, oats, lignite coal, and even waste biomass [29]. In our study, purslane leaves will be employed as a natural source for the green synthesis of CQDs, as it is a source of a power food because of its high nutritive and antioxidant properties [30]. Purslane leaves are edible, have more omega-3 fatty acids, excellent source of vitamins A, C, and some B-complex vitamins. As well as dietary minerals.

The sensors based on quantum dots coating on the QCM electrode recently attracted an intention for the detection of VOCs and humidity. Li et al. [31] have used PbS quantum dots spin-coated onto the acoustic sensor for the detection of NO2 gas. The sensor showed fast response of NO2, good selectivity and sensitivity as well as short recovery times. In addition, ZnO quantum dots/QCM [32] was used for humidity detection. The sensor showed good performance, stability, reproducibility, response time and recovery time. Recently, Qi et al. [33] studied a QCM sensor for humidity based on graphene quantum dots-chitosan. The sensor exhibited high response sensitivity, short response/recovery time, excellent reproducibility, and long-term stability. The introduction of quantum dots into chitosan enhanced the mechanical stiffness of the chitosan film that improved the QCM sensor performance.

In the present paper, we report a facile, scalable and economical green method to prepare CQDs from purslane leaves in one-step hydrothermal process with a good fluorescence efficiency. The one-step hydrothermal route is utilized for the synthesis process due to the simplicity and rapidity of the technique, mild and controlled reaction conditions, and the cost effectiveness of this route as compared to other techniques. In addition, this method provides the production of CQDs from green precursors without using toxic chemicals or strong acids. The CQDs synergistically combines the π-rich-graphitized carbon with a considerable content of oxygen functionalities; thus, it shows a good sensing affinity in vapor molecular discrimination of vaporized formaldehyde among other interfering organic vapors, such as amines, alcohols, and chlorinated and aromatic hydrocarbons. A simple, sensitive, rapid, and reproducible sensor of CQDs thin film drop-coated on the QCM electrode show a good sensitivity toward formaldehyde vapors at room temperature. The adsorption kinetics of formaldehyde vapors onto the CQDs film coating on QCM were carefully investigated and discussed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green synthesis of carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of CQDs was conducted typically by collecting 10 g of fresh purslane leaves from agricultural land in Egypt and washed thoroughly with water and then dried in the presence of sunlight. Then, the leaves were crushed to get a fine powder and 5 g of this brown powder were suspended in distilled water (100 mL). Further, the suspension was poured into a 250 mL-sized Teflon-lined autoclave that was kept in the oven for 4 h at 150 °C. The resulting dark brown color solution indicates the formation of CQDs. These CQDs were then collected and purified to remove the large particles by centrifugation at 12,000 rpm followed by dialysis using 3.5 kDa dialysis bags. Accordingly, the preparation process of CQDs is expressed by Fig. 1 .

The absolute quantum yield (QY) of the CQDs derived from purslane leaves dispersion were determined using a spectrofluorometer (Hitachi F-2700) equipped with an integrating sphere. 3 mL of aqueous dispersion of sample was sealed in a quartz cell (10 mm × 10 mm), and its maximal absorption intensity was controlled to be less than 0.1. The same volume of water was used as a blank sample. The excitation wavelength was set at 360 nm. The scattering spectral range of blank and sample was set from 310 nm to 360 nm, and the emission spectral range was from 400 to 650 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of purslane carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were examined under JEOL JEM-2100F. A PerkinElmer Spectrum BX spectrometer was used to measure the FT-IR spectra of CQDs. A GNR APD–2000 PRO diffractometer with Cu Ka radiation (40 KV, 30 mA) was used in a step scan mode to get X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns. A UV spectrometer, UVD-2960 (Labomed Inc.) was employed to record the UV–Vis absorption spectra. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the samples were obtained by a Hitachi F-2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer with light source Xenon lamp of 150 W and the excitation light source was adjusted at 360 nm. The fluorescence decay was recorded by fluorescence lifetime measuring system (Horiba Fluoro Cube 3000U-EYM1) and the excitation light source was adjusted at 372 nm. UV Lamp (6 Watt, 0.08 Amps, 365 nm, Model–Upland, CA, USA) was used for irradiation. The XPS spectra of the synthesized CQDs were obtained using X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Multilab2000, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), operated with Al Kα radiation at 20 kV at 5 mA. The binding energy shift was calibrated using the C1s level at 285.0 eV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quartz crystal microbalance gas sensor set up
ce:section.ce:para: A 5 MHz AT-cut quartz crystal, coated with gold electrodes from both sides, was used [34,35]. The details of the QCM apparatus use for the frequency measurements was described elsewhere [21]. A GW frequency counter (Model GFC-8055G) was used to determine the resonance frequency of the crystal. Fig. 2 is the schematic diagram of a laboratory-setup QCM measuring system setup that was designed and fabricated for the detection of VOCs.

Prior to coat the CQDs sample, QCM electrode was cleaned with ethanol. Then, the electrode was dried in an oven, and its fundamental frequency (f 0) was recorded to be used later for determining the mass of deposited CQDs onto the surface of the QCM gold electrode using the Sauerbrey equation (Eqn. (1)). An enhanced CQDs-QCM sensor was prepared by drop-coating a 50 μl of the CQDs suspension onto the gold surface of the QCM gold electrode at room temperature, then left undisturbed for 30 min. Then the coated electrodes were dried in a vacuum drying oven for 30 min at 50 °C. Afterward, the CQDs-coated electrode was connected to the QCM circuit, and the frequency was recorded as f 1 for which a stable response indicates a good adherence of CQDs film to the surface of the QCM gold electrode. The frequency changes were calculated and the mass of the deposited CQDs film onto the QCM electrode (μg cm−2) was estimated using the Sauerbrey equation (Eqn. (1)).

The sensing of CQDs for VOCs vapor, such as formaldehyde, ethanol, methanol, triethylamine, methylene chloride, chloroform, carbon tetrachloride, and benzene, were studied at room temperature (∼24 ± 1 °C). All the measurements were carried out in a completely sealed polyethylene cell with an internal volume of 140 mL. After obtaining a steady state that indicates a complete adsorption, the CQDs-coated QCM electrodes saturated with the analyte vapor was exposed to hot dry air to desorb the analyte and recover the frequency of the electrode to its initial value indicating the full vapor desorption. The prepared QCM sensor electrode coated with CQDs was stored in a vacuum desiccator in a good way to be reusable for several injection experiments. The frequency-time dependence was automatically recorded by a PC during the sequential injection of the various VOCs.

A small volume of the liquid analyte was injected into the test bottle using a Hamilton microliter syringe and the liquid was totally evaporated inside the cell and the concentrations of injected vapor concentration (mg L−1) was obtained by applying the following equation [15,36]:
(2)
C =
ρ
V l
V
where, ρ is the relevant density of the liquid substance in mg L−1, C is the analyte concentration (mg L−1), V refers to the volume of the testing vessel in L, and V l refers to the volume of liquid samples in L.

The responses (Δf) were calculated using the equation: Δf = f
air – f gas, where f gas and f air is the frequency measured in target gases and air, respectively. The graphs between Δf and injected gas concentrations were plotted and showed linear relationships. The slope coefficient is defined as sensitivity of the gas sensor: S = Δf/(ΔConc.), in which ΔConc. refers to the concentration range of the chemical-analyte vapors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterizations of carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Purslane is a leafy green and edible vegetable that is grown in many areas of the world and is known scientifically as Portulaca oleracea. In addition, purslane contains a lot of vitamins and dietary minerals. Furthermore, purslane leaves can act as a natural source for the synthesis of CQDs. The CQDs were successfully green synthesized through a one–step hydrothermal treatment. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the purslane CQDs aqueous dispersion is shown in Fig. 3 (a). Two predominant UV absorption peaks were observed at 275 and 320 nm, which can be assigned to π–π∗ transition of CC bonds and n–π∗ transition of CO bonds in CQDs, respectively [37]. Upon UV light irradiation (365 nm), a strong green colored mission was observed, revealing that purslane CQDs possess strong green luminescence (Fig. 3(a)). This significant blue shift is usually attributed to size-dependence of the electronic structure effects, in which the decrease of π-electron density leads to a longer energy gap for the surface states, thus decrease the amount of lower-wavelength emission of CQDs.

The band gap (E
g ) was calculated to be 5.71 eV from Tauc plot (Fig. 3(b)) by plotting (αhν)n versus energy (eV) by applying the following Tauc and Davis–Mott relation and drawing the tangent line on the curve where α = 0 to calculate E g [38].
(3) (αhν)n = K (hν – E
g )
where, “α” is absorption coefficient, “hν” is incident photon energy, “K” is energy independent constant and E
g is the optical band gap [39]. The PL of CQDs can be attributed to the electronic transitions from the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital and the highest occupied molecular orbital. As a matter of fact, with the decrease of the size of QDs, the emission wavelength of QDs is blue-shifted and PL intensity increases, because of the lower density of π-electron system which emits strong longer-wavelength fluorescence [40]. The PL spectra of the purslane CQDs were obtained after their excitation at different wavelengths ranging from 300 nm to 500 nm. Fig. 3(c) shows that the presence of a red shift in the PL emission peak from 465 to 545 nm by varying the wavelength of excitation from 300 nm to 500 nm. However, the increase in the wavelength of PL emission peak (red shift) is not very clear, possibly due to the presence of more than one particle size and different emissive sites on the CQDs surface [41]. This defect sites result in the quenching of the transition that corresponds to the red shift of the main peak [42]. Fig. S1 shows the excitation spectra of the synthesized CQDs. Fig. 3(d) shows the luminescence decay curve of the synthesized purslane CQDs. In addition, the lifetime data were fitted well to a triple exponential function. Its emission properties obtained by time-resolved PL analysis of the CQDs dispersion, and their average fluorescence lifetimes was determined to be 3.7 and 0.46 ns, indicating that the excitons are stable and do not find non-radiative emission channels within the QDs on these shorter time scales. The lowest lifetime attributed to intrinsic state and higher lifetime correspond to surface state [43].

The absolute emission QY of the CQDs was calculated by applying the relative method and measured by using quinine sulfate in sulfuric acid solution with a quantum yield of 0.546 as a reference [44]. Five concentrations of the CQDs and quinine sulfate were prepared all of which the absorbance values were less than 0.1. The fluorescence intensities were plotted versus absorbance and the slope of the CQDs and quinine sulfate was determined (Fig. S2) to be 3976 and 17,193, respectively. The QY was calculated according to the following equation [45]:
(4)
ϕ u
=
ϕ s
×
m u
m s
×
n s
n u
where, ϕu and ϕs are the fluorescence quantum yields of the CQDs and quinine sulfate, respectively. m
u and m
s are the slopes of the curves obtained for the CQDs and quinine sulfate, respectively. n
u and n
s are the refractive indices for the CQDs and the standard, respectively. For these aqueous solutions, n u/ns = 1. The emission QY value was found to be as high as 12.5% using an integrating sphere, a rather pleasing value compared to those previously reported for CQDs derived from watermelon and apple, strawberry and carrot juice as shown in Table S1. The enhanced photoresponse compared to previous reports is assumed to originate from the presence of pyridinic–N and pyrrolic–N groups, which lowers electronic energy states that direct the photoluminescence and avoid non-radiative recombination.

The structure of the synthesized purslane CQDs was investigated by XRD analysis. As seen in Fig. 4 (a), the dominant amorphous structure of the purslane CQDs is reflected in the presence of a broad diffraction peak centered at about 2θ = 22° in the XRD pattern that belongs to interlayer lattice planes of (002) and results from a highly turbostratic-type staking of a graphitic carbon that existed in a random fashion [39,46], due to the introduction of amine and oxygen–containing groups during the synthesis of CQDs [47].

The morphology and size of the CQDs were characterized by TEM observations. Clearly, TEM image reveals that clear dark spots of CQDs are observed and indicative of the formation of localized clusters of carbon atoms as shown in Fig. 4(b). However, the bright field images of amorphous carbons are seldom distinct because of the low contrast between CQDs and carbon coated copper of TEM grids. From the particle size distributions of CQDs (inset of Fig. 4(b)), these apparently amorphous CQDS clusters are nearly spherical morphology with an average diameter of 6.14 nm. The dispersity index of the CQDs was calculated from the square of the standard deviation divided by the average diameter of CQDs [48]. The average diameter and the standard deviation were found to be 6.14 nm and 1.657, respectively. Hence the dispersity index was calculated to be 0.07 that indicates the monodispersity of the synthesized CQDs. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image further confirms that the purslane CQDs possesses randomly assembled nanometer-sized graphic carbon structures (Fig. 4(c)). The layered structure can also be confirmed by selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (inset of Fig. 2(c)), and the presence of a diffused spots indicates the periodic arrangement of the graphitic layers. The broadening nature of the spots indicates the low crystallinity degree of CQDs structure. The high-magnification TEM image shows the ordered atomic arrangement with a lattice spacing (d) of 0.31 nm, which is well-consistent with the (002) plane of sp 2-graphitized carbon interlayers (Fig. 4(d and e)) [49]. Such amorphous CQD morphology is widely observed in previous studies [50].

The FT-IR measurement was used to investigate the chemical composition and the surface functional groups of prepared purslane CQDs as shown in Fig. 5 (a). The CQDs displays a characteristic band at 3462 cm−1 which is assigned to O–H bonded to CQDs surface and the adsorbed water, as well [39,51]. In addition, two absorption bands located at 2997 cm−1 and 2863 cm−1 correspond to C–H stretching vibration [50], while the absorption bands at 2351 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibrations of C–N in the aromatic rings [51]. Three representative absorption bands appeared at 1642, 1390 and 1050 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of CO, C–O (carboxy) and C–O (alkoxy) bonds in the carboxyl groups of CQDs, respectively [37,52]. Clearly, the presence of these oxygen-containing groups, including carbonyl and carboxyl, endows the synthesized purslane CQDs with good solubility in water [53]. The FTIR investigations suggested functional hydroxyl, carboxylic, and carbonyl groups, derived from organic moieties in purslane are introduced onto the surfaces of the CQDs under hydrothermal conditions [50].

In order to understand the details of the relative chemical composition on the surface and electronic states of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen in CQDs, XPS measurements were collected and analyzed very carefully as shown in Fig. 5(b–e). Clearly, wide-scan XPS spectrum shows three main intensive peaks observed at 283.1, 399.2 and 531.6 eV, which are attributed to C1s, N1s and O1s states, respectively, demonstrating that CQDs mainly contain C, N and O elements (Fig. 5(b), Table S2). As shown in Fig. 5(c–e), high resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s and O1s are plotted. The enlarged C1s spectrum of CQDs (Fig. 5(c) can be deconvoluted into five peaks. Two peaks denoted as C1 and C2 are assigned to a graphitic sp 2–carbon structure (CC) and an amorphous sp 3–carbon (C–C), respectively. Three predominant peaks C3, C4 and C5 are characteristic of sp 2–carbon directly bonded to nitrogen atoms (CN), oxygen in the hydroxyl configuration (C–OH) and carbonyl groups (CO), respectively [54]. In Fig. 5(d), the N1s spectra of CQDs was deconvoluted into three peaks. From the N1s spectrum, a low content of carbon bonded nitrogen in CQDs is observed. The N1 peak is ascribed to pyridinic–N [55]. Further, deconvoluted N1s peak shows a major component denoted as N2, which is attributed to pyrrolic–N. The N3 peak represents quaternary nitrogen, graphitic–N that is steadily incorporated into graphitic carbon framework of CQDs and replaces carbon atoms within a graphene plane, indicating that a graphitic–like carbon structure was developed. Clearly, almost all doped N exist as a pair of π–electron with pyridinic and pyrrolic carbon rings (Table S3). The sp2–nitrogen (CN) may act as an electron donor at the carbon edge layers to facilitate the diffusion of guest molecules for chemisorption with volatile polar hydrocarbons.

Interestingly, the presence of N atoms in the carbon framework creates structural defects of unsaturated carbon atoms at carbon edges which are very active to react with physically absorbed oxygen and form oxygen functional groups when exposed to air [54]. Fig. 5(e) shows the deconvoluted high–resolution O1s spectrum. The O1 peak is assigned to physically absorbed oxygen (34.05%). Two additional peaks O2 and O3 correspond to the presence of oxygen atoms having double bonds to carbon atoms (−CO carbonyl, 43.09%), and hydroxyl C–OH/C–O–C groups (22.86%) on the surface of the CQDs, respectively [56–58]. As a matter of fact, our CQDs possess a considerable content of oxygen arising from the carbon–oxygen bonds in the C1s deconvolution (Fig. 5(c)). Despite that developed oxygen function groups are non–basic, CQDs effectively became an impressive sensing material towards polar vapor analytes through green synthesis and simple carbonization of purslane. Meanwhile, a limited amount of Na and K can be detected, which may come from trace minerals in purslane plant. While Si element may originate from the glass substrate during XPS measurements [59].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quartz crystal microbalance sensor for formaldehyde vapors
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the unique properties of CQDs, the sensing capabilities of the CQDs were demonstrated toward formaldehyde vapor using a QCM sensor technique. The research interest in studying the sensing properties of CQDs is based also on the synergetic cooperation between the developed graphitic carbon and the functional groups that present in CQDs that are expected to dramatically enhance the vapor discrimination properties to selectively detect an individual toxic chemical analyte [60]. In addition, since the CQDs have large surface area that provide large number of active sites for the absorption of formaldehyde gas molecules and hence increases gas response. Therefore, CQDs, synthesized through simple and effective hydrothermal method, were utilized as a sensitive coating film on the QCM electrode for the selective detection of hazardous volatile formaldehyde, that is very toxic to humans. For this purpose, an enhanced QCM sensor drop–coated with CQDs was fabricated.

The CQDs coated QCM electrode was treated with various concentrations of formaldehyde vapor. Upon the exposure to the initial formaldehyde concentration, the frequency of the CQDs coated QCM electrode decreased, which indicates the adsorption of formaldehyde vapors onto the CQDs according to Eqn. (1). The frequency changes, due to the adsorption of formaldehyde vapors onto the film, were recorded as a function of time. The relationship between Δf versus time at different concentrations of formaldehyde (2.9–23.2 mg L−1) is shown in Fig. 6 (a). Δf linearly increased with increasing time till the steady state. After attaining the equilibrium, the QCM frequency was back shifted to its initial value after drying up the CQDs film saturated with formaldehyde molecules by using hot dry air. In addition, it can be observed that as the formaldehyde vapor concentration increases, the magnitude of the QCM response increases as well. A calibration curve of Δf versus the formaldehyde vapor concentrations was plotted and linear correlation was obtained (R = 0.991 and slope (sensitivity) = 42.61 Hz mg−1 L), Fig. 6(b).

The interaction and the sensing abilities of CQDs film-coated QCM electrode with other organic analyte such as methanol, ethanol and triethylamine vapors were tested. To remove any changes due to the use of different film thicknesses between experiments, the frequency changes of the next experiments were normalized by the mass of the films in the active QCM area [61].

The CQDs–coated QCM electrode was exposed to various concentrations of methanol, ethanol and triethylamine vapors and the frequency changes due to the adsorption uptake of these vapors onto the CQDs film were recorded as a function of time for different concentrations as shown in Figs. S3(a, c, and e), respectively. Calibration curves of Δf as a function of methanol, ethanol and triethylamine vapors concentrations were plotted and good linear relationships were obtained as shown in Figs. S3(b, d, and f). The different characteristics (including the correlation coefficient and sensitivity) for the detection of different analytes using CQDs film coated QCM electrode was calculated and given in Table 1 .

Xu et al. [62] detect formaldehyde vapor based on a thin layer of copper complex [Cu(DDS)2(Cl)2 (MeOH)2] deposited onto the QCM electrode. The sensing test results based on thermodynamic investigations indicated that the sensor exhibited good sensitivity for formaldehyde vapor due to a reversible interaction between the aldehyde group of formaldehyde molecules and the amino group of the copper complex. Furthermore, Yu et al. [63] detected formaldehyde vapor using TiO2 nanofibers/polyethyleneimine–coated QCM electrode. The coating layers were sensitive to formaldehyde molecules and the interaction was attributed to the chemical nucleophilic addition reaction between formaldehyde molecules and amine groups of polyethyleneimine. Similar studies were performed using polyethyleneimine/poly (vinyl alcohol) nanofibrous membranes [64] and polyethyleneimine (PEI)-multiwalled carbon nanotubes nanocomposite [65] as coatings on the QCM electrodes. The chemical interaction between amine functional groups of PEI and the formaldehyde gas molecules was the basis of the sensing mechanism of the constructed sensor toward formaldehyde vapors [64].

Huang et al. [66] demonstrated that the weak van der Waals interactions are the reason for the absorption of formaldehyde molecule on zinc oxide quantum dots/graphene nanocomposites. In another investigation [67], the interaction between the hydroxyl groups attached to the surface of sensing elements with formaldehyde molecules was proposed and laid to form weak and reversible charged intermediate. Generally, many possible interactions, such as chemical nucleophilic interaction, hydrogen bonding and van der Waals forces between the CQDs and formaldehyde gas molecule could be proposed, many sensing materials, techniques and mechanisms of formaldehyde based on QCM sensor were summarized in Table S4. From these data, it is reasonable to suggest that the sensing mechanism of the interaction of the formaldehyde vapor with purslane CQDs could be based on the interaction between formaldehyde molecules and the amine and oxygen functional groups such as the hydroxyl and carboxylic groups of the CQDs (according to XPS results) as well as the van der Waals forces between the high sp
2 hybridization of the CQDs and formaldehyde molecules, as illustrated in Fig. 7 .

Methanol and ethanol contain alkyl groups with positive inductive effect that may result in less interaction and less probability of hydrogen bond formation with hydroxyl, carboxyl and the amino groups on the CQDs in addition to van der Waal interactions. The difference between the sensitivity of the sensor towards formaldehyde, alcohols and triethylamine molecules is the presence of the chemical nucleophilic interaction in case of formaldehyde.

In light of the good sensitivity, QCDs drop-coated QCM sensor is effectively integrated as electrode material for enhanced sensing activity toward volatile formaldehyde as shown in Fig. 8 (a). Considering the sensing uptake of vaporized formaldehyde, the oxygen-rich CQDs functions as a remarkable sensing material for formaldehyde vapor compared to previously reported structurally designed materials-based QCM sensors, such as graphene oxide [68], multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) and their composites [69,70], nanoporous polymers [71,72], polymeric materials [64,73], chitosan composite [74], calixarene derivatives [75] and copper-manganese composite oxide [76] (Table S5). The sensing response of the CQDs–based sensor toward formaldehyde was investigated by exposing the CQDs film-coated QCM electrode to various VOCs, including alcohols, triethylamine, benzene and chlorinated hydrocarbons (methylene chloride, chloroform and carbon tetrachloride) of the same concentration (10 mg L−1). The responses plot of the sensor toward the different introduced analytes is shown in Fig. 8(a). The CQDs-based sensor exhibited Δf of 721 Hz toward formaldehyde while the same sensor shows Δf values of 166, 142, 138, 119, 103, 93, 74 and 298 Hz toward triethylamine, methanol, chloroform, ethanol, carbon tetrachloride, methylene chloride, benzene and water, respectively. By comparing the aforementioned responses, it can be found that the CQDs–based sensor exhibited the highest sensitivity toward formaldehyde vapor. However, the fabricated sensor, at the same time, was less sensitive to volatile triethylamine, chloroform, methanol, ethanol, carbon tetrachloride, methylene chloride, benzene and water. These results prove that the QCM electrode coated with purslane CQDs has a good sensing response for formaldehyde among many other interfering organic analytes. This result indicates that in spite of our designed sensor exhibits larger sensing response for volatile formaldehyde (712 Hz) regardless of the low limit of detection (LOD), its LOD is still challenging for practical applications. Although, our designed CQDs-based QCM sensor does show slight response for low concentrations of formaldehyde, and in the future research, we will consider the sensor activity at the lowest test concentrations by conforming to utilize CQDs with tailored properties and chemical functionalization or optimizing the experimental devices.

Different types of chemical-vapors are chosen to evaluate the gas selectivity of CQDs to vaporized formaldehyde. As shown in Fig. 8(a), CQDs-based QCM sensor exhibits a good selectivity to formaldehyde vapor. When CQDs coated QCM sensor is exposed to various chemical-vapors (all at 10 mg L−1), it shows the highest response to formaldehyde of about 721 Hz. The frequency change (Δf) to benzene is the lowest, it exhibits only 53 Hz. The selectivity can be calculated based on Fig. 8(b) and Eq. (2) [77] as follows:
(5)
σ =
R i
g a s
∑
i = 1
N
R i

× 100

In the formula, the value of R
i
gas is the response of the QCM sensor to a particular gas, where selectivity values, are plotted against the gas types at a concentration of 10 mg L−1 and at 25 °C, Fig. 8(b) is obtained. Generally, a good selectivity to HCHO was observed over various volatile organic compounds (Fig. 8(b)). The magnitude of response to 10 mg L−1 of HCHO is larger than that to the same concentration of interferences with other volatiles such as methanol, ethanol, trimethylamine, methylene chloride, chloroform, carbon tetrachloride, benzene, and water. The CQDs-based sensor has the highest sensing response to formaldehyde vapors at approximately 39%. This good selectivity to formaldehyde vapors should be attributed to the strong hydrogen bonding interaction and existence of van der Waals forces between the high sp
2 hybridization of the CQDs and formaldehyde molecules.

The effect of humidity on formaldehyde detection is not neglected when the sensors work at the room environment, therefore interference test is necessary. The sensing results of formaldehyde in comparison of water with same concentration of 10 mg L−1 are presented in Fig. 8(a). This result indicating that in spite of the high-water content in the air humidified working cell may cause interference, the interferential signal may slightly affect the sensor response compared with the response to formaldehyde vapor because of the high sensor selectivity as shown in Fig. 8(b). Since CQDs is not a microporous material, it does not inevitably adsorb small molecules, such as CO2 and H2O. However, the effect of this simple physical adsorption is very weak, and that is why the adsorption of water increases and the response is increased by only about 296 Hz. But we have to confess that water does interfere with this sensor, and in the next research, we will reduce the interference of humidity by optimizing the sensing conditions.

For exploring the other interference chemical-vapor analytes, the QCM sensor based-CQDs was tested using air as carrier gas to test the sensor’s signal when it was exposed to different concentrations of formaldehyde vapor with seven interfering vapors (10 mg L−1), Fig. 8(c). As shown in Fig. 8(d), there are some deviations of baseline, indicating that the sensor presents a relatively good selectivity formaldehyde vapors in the presence of various interfering gases except the formaldehyde-water mixture that exhibited large interference as compared to the other mixtures. In summary, the CQDs-based QCM sensor is still reliable for detecting volatile formaldehyde when it exposes in the air regardless of the interfering components except water.

To check the adsorption affinity, the enhanced CQDs-based QCM sensor (m’ = 70.4 μg) was exposed to different VOCs, such as formaldehyde, triethylamine and aliphatic alcohol of the same concentration (10 mg L−1) using the same modified QCM electrode, and the time-dependent Δfs for different VOCs were plotted as shown in Fig. 9 (a). After injection of formaldehyde, the frequency exhibited an immediate change (Δf = 1280 Hz), indicating the distinguished sensing affinity toward formaldehyde, which is almost 4.7 times and 6.4 times higher than that in case of triethylamine and ethanol (Fig. 9(a)). Further, aliphatic alcohols show similar trends to those in response to formaldehyde and the variation of the Δf increases with increasing the polarity and acidity. On the other hand, compared to aliphatic alcohols, the increased relatively high sensing of CQDs toward triethylamine can be ascribed to its high basic nature. Although, the sensing affinity of CQDs toward formaldehyde is remarkably high, the sensor can adsorb triethylamine vapor faster than that in case of volatile formaldehyde. Consequently, pseudo-first order mass transfer can be employed to describe the adsorption uptake of the different organic vapors onto the same CQDs film. In this regard, the concentration of adsorbed VOCs onto CQDs film is proposed to be constant, and the adsorption of vapors is controlled by the concentration gradient through the CQDs layer. Therefore, the VOCs adsorption kinetics may be considered as a pseudo-first-order mass transfer between the film sorption sites and the vapor phase, which can be investigated by monitoring Δf upon exposure to volatile molecules at different time. The rate of analyte vapor uptake can be obtained [78] by calculating the pseudo–first order rate constant (k) according to the following the equation:
(6)
Δ
f t
Δ
f ∞
= 1 −
e
− k t
where, Δf t is the change in the frequency due to the adsorption of organic vapor onto CQDs film at time t and Δf
∞ is the change in the frequency at the end of the adsorption process of the equilibrium state. These two parameters can be expressed as:
Δf t = f CQDs– f t and Δf ∞ = f CQDs– f ∞
where, f CQDs is the initial frequency, f t is the frequency during the exposure process at time t, while Δf t and Δf
∞ describe the analyte weight uptake at time t and at equilibrium, respectively. The pseudo–first order rate constant can be obtained from the straight–line slope of ln (1–Δf t /Δf
∞ ) versus time.

The pseudo-first order kinetic (k) were determined using Eqn. (4) from the gradient of the kinetic plot of ln (1-Δf t/Δf ∞) versus t (Fig. 9(b)) and are listed in Table 2 . As clearly seen in Fig. 9(b), a rapid initial uptake for formaldehyde molecules followed by a linear regression is obtained, and the estimated k values for formaldehyde, triethylamine, methanol and ethanol are 2.6 × 10−3 s−1, 2.75 × 10−3 s−1, 2.49 × 10−3 s−1 and 2.27 × 10−3 s−1, respectively. Based on these results, it is showed that the rate of uptake of formaldehyde vapor by the CQDs film proceeded more rapidly than alcohols and relatively slower than amine probably due to the low vapor pressure of formaldehyde, which were calculated at 25 °C as 1.826 and 1.207 for triethylamine and formaldehyde, respectively [79]. The Δf and the rate constant values for all analytes’ vapors are presented and compared in Table 2. Since formaldehyde possesses the highest Δf value and a high-rate constant as well, both of dynamic (kinetic) and static (Δf) values can be considered as a basis of selectivity.

Reproducibility is a critically important factor for assessing gas sensor. In order to check the reversibility and the reproducibility of our designed sensor, the CQDs film-coated QCM electrode (65.286 μg) was subjected to formaldehyde vapor concentration (5.8 mg L−1). After the sensor properly adsorbed the formaldehyde molecules and the sensor reached the steady state equilibrium condition, Δf of 385 Hz was attained. To regenerate the CQDs-coated QCM saturated with vapors, the electrode was subjected to hot air dryer for 5 min until achieving full desorption of formaldehyde molecules. The complete reversibility of the CQDs-based sensor was assessed by the alternative adsorption-desorption experiments several times as presented in Fig. 10 . The fabricated QCM sensor clearly exhibits excellent reversibility, implying that CQDs-based QCM sensor can be employed to detect a wide variety of VOCs several times without any serious damage. The vapor analyte is rapidly desorbed, and the frequency underwent to its initial value within few minutes, demonstrating that the binding of formaldehyde molecules by CQDs was occurred mainly via physical adsorption.

In summary, we have successfully reported a scalable and green method to synthesis CQDs from purslane. The synthesized CQDs were characterized by different techniques. The band gap was calculated from the UV–Vis absorption spectra and Tauc plot. The synthesized CQDs with an average diameter of 6.14 nm are amorphous in nature and possessed a considerable content of oxygen arising from the carbon-oxygen bonds, which was a key characteristic in the high sensing activity and selectivity of CQDs toward vaporized formaldehyde molecules. The CQDs film drop-coated QCM electrode further explored a distinguished selectivity toward formaldehyde with a high sensitivity that reaches 42.61 Hz mg−1 L among many other interfering organic analytes at room temperature. The designed QCM-based sensor showed good reproducibility and reversibility. The adsorption of formaldehyde follows pseudo-first order kinetic model and the rate of vapors uptake was carefully investigated and discussed. Our work explains in detail how to design a QCM formaldehyde sensor, which has in-depth scientific research and application prospects. However, as nothing is perfect, there is still a little defect of the interfere of humidity although the response of water is much lower than the response of formaldehyde, therefore, in the future research, we will focus on reducing the influence of humidity on the sensor by optimizing the conditions in sensing application. We will further explore the potential use of QCM sensor integrated with CQDs in developing an efficient gas sensor to detect hazardous VOCs from environmental atmospheric emissions at room temperature in the next research study.
